text:
Admission Date:  1988-04-17              Discharge Date:   1990-09-29

Date of Birth:  1983-07-06             Sex:   M

Service: CARDIOTHORACIC

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Gina
Chief Complaint:
Neck tightness with exertion

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1988-04-17 Cardiac catheterization
1970-10-29 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft x 2 (LIMA to LAD, SVG to
OM)

History of Present Illness:
This is a 72 y.o. man with a history of HTN and 6-8 months of
exertional neck tightness with an abnormal stress test.
[**Month/Day/Year **] Cardiac Cath which revealed 2 vessel disease. Referred
for coronary bypass surgery.

Past Medical History:
Hypertension, Hypercholesterolemia, Williams phenomenon,
Diverticulosis, s/p removal of benign colon polyps

Social History:
Patient is widowed and lives alone. He has three biological
children and 2 step children. Patient is retired, previously
working as a bus driver. His last job was in the flooring
business. Patient smoked 2.5-3 packs a day for approximately 30
years, quitting in 1993

Family History:
Father with heart problems, no specifics. He passed away at age
72. Denies family history of CAD. Mother, brother and
grandmother with DM. No history of CVA.

Physical Exam:
VS 98.6 123/56 59 18 98%RA
Gen: Well-appearing, NAD.
Skin: No rashes, or lesions.
HEENT: PERRL. Pink, moist oral mucosa without lesions.
CV: RRR. Normal S1 and S2. No M/R/G.
Pulm: CTA bilaterally.
Abd: Soft, nontender, nondistended. Normoactive bowel sounds.
Ext: Warm, well-perfused. No edema or varicosities
Neuro: A&Ox3, non-focal, MAE

Pertinent Results:
06-09 Cardiac cath: LMCA heavy calcification with distal taper
30%, LAD origin 95% heavily calcified lesion, distal LAD is a
good target, LCx with origin 60% calcified lesion, RCA with
modest diffuse calcification.
08-07 Echo: PRE-BYPASS: A patent foramen ovale is present. A
left-to-right shunt across the interatrial septum is seen at
rest. Regional left ventricular wall motion is normal. Overall
left ventricular systolic function is low normal (LVEF 50-55%).
Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal.
There are simple atheroma in the aortic arch. The descending
thoracic aorta is mildly dilated. There are complex
(>4mm)atheroma in the descending thoracic aorta. There are three
aortic valve leaflets. There is no aortic valve stenosis. Mild
to moderate (09-22+) aortic regurgitation is seen. The aortic
regurgitation jet is eccentric. Mild (1+) mitral regurgitation
is seen. POST-BYPASS: Pt is being A paced and is on an infusion
of phenylephrine. Biventricular systolic function is preserved.
Other findings are unchanged
05-27 CXR: Small bilateral pleural effusion has increased. Mild
enlargement of the postoperative cardiomediastinal silhouette is
stable. There is no pneumothorax. Lungs clear. Small collection
of retrosternal gas and fluid at the level of the sternal angle
is a common postoperative finding, but not necessarily
pathologic.

Brief Hospital Course:
As mentioned in the HPI, Mr. Jones Williams cardiac cath on
06-09 which revealed severe 2 vessel disease. He was admitted
following cath for coronary bypass surgery. He [**Month/Day (1) 1834**] usual
pre-operative work-up prior to surgery. On 08-07 he was brought
to the operating room where he [**Month/Day (4) 1834**] a coronary artery
bypass graft x 2. Please see operative report for surgical
details. He tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to
the CSRU for invasive monitoring in stable condition. Later on
op day sedation was weaned. He awoke neurologically intact and
was extubated. He did require some FFP and platelets for post-op
bleeding. On post-op day one he was started on Beta blockers and
diuretics. He was gently diuresed towards his pre-op weight.
Later on this day he was transferred to the SDU. On post-op day
two he had episode of rapid Atrial Fibrillation. This was
treated accordingly with good response. Also on this day his
chest tubes were removed. On post-op day three he again had
episode of AFIB. Amiodarone was started and eventually Coumadin
as well. He continued to have episodes or AFIB and atrial
bigeminy and was be discharged with Amiodarone and Coumadin.
Epicardial pacing wires were removed on post-op day three.
Throughout post-op course his HCT remained low but he was
asymptomatic. He was treated with Folic acid, Iron and VIT C. He
continued to make clinical improvements and appeared to be doing
well by POD #6 and was discharged home with VNA services and the
appropriate follow-up appointments.

Medications on Admission:
Nifedical 30mg daily every morning, Lovastatin 20mg daily every
morning, Atenolol 25mg daily every afternoon (recently started
after stress test), Aspirin 81mg daily every morning, Folic acid
800mcg daily every afternoon, B12 200mcg every afternoon, Plavix
75mg daily every afternoon (recently started after stress test),
MVI QD

Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
[**Company 1519**]

Discharge Diagnosis:
Coronary Artery Disease s/p Coronary Artery Bypass Graft x 2
Post-Op Atrial Fibrillation
PMH: Hypertension, Hypercholesterolemia, Diverticulosis,
Williams Phenomenon, s/p Removal of benign colon polyp

Discharge Condition:
Good

Discharge Instructions:
28 shower, no baths or swimming
No creams, lotions, powders, or ointments to incisions
No driving for approximately one month
No lifting more than 10 pounds for 10 weeks
Monitor wounds for infection - redness, drainage, or increased
pain
Report any fever greater than 101
Report any weight gain of greater than 2 pounds in 24 hours or 5
pounds in a week
Please call with any questions or concerns

Followup Instructions:
Dr. Lutz in 4 weeks
Dr. Hunter in 05-10 weeks
Dr. Hunter in 09-22 weeks
